item.title: Member Family Day: Animal Intelligence
item.description:
Saturday, April 11, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

SAM members, don't have a room with a view booked this spring break? Kick off your staycation with A Room for Animal Intelligence. Get your free tickets to visit the museum on April 11 \(or anytime through April 19\) and enjoy enriching activities. This is a whole new way of seeing the animal world curated by Pam McClusky. Come face-to-face with 100 animals from around the globe, rendered at jaw- dropping scale. We're talking the largest wolf, mouse, and mosquito you've ever laid eyes on. The kids won't stop talking about it-and honestly, neither will you. Grab a bite from the Shooby Doo pop-up café \(11 am-3 pm\), listen to live jazz curated by Seattle Jazz Fellowship \(11 am-2 pm\), and join a public collection tour that includes a stop in A Room for Animal Intelligence \(11:30 am\). 10 am-5 pm Build a Beast Brotman Forum Build your very own beast using hand-carved animal stamps created by artist Brian Lane. Pen Pal with an Animal A Room for Animal Intelligence gallery Choose your favorite animal in the exhibition to be pen pals with and write to them using the stationary provided in the gallery. Who knows! You might just hear back . . . Wolf Haven International Booth Brotman Forum Visit the Wolf Haven International booth to learn more about wolves and the organization's efforts to restore near-extinct species. Animal-themed Scavenger Hunt A Room for Animal Intelligence Pick up a scavenger hunt created by SAM Docent, David Turner, from the Ticketing Desk and embark on your adventure to discover the wit, wonder, and wisdom of A Room for Animal Intelligence! 10:30 am | Complimentary Pastries for Members Arnold Board Room Start your morning with a delicious pastry made by Shooby Doo Catering \(while supplies last\). 11-11:30 am | Illustrated Storytime with Julie Kim Arnold Board Room Bring your little one to experience the artistic magic of an illustrated animal storytime by artist Julie Kim. 11:30 am-noon | Selfies with Sammy the Camel A Room for Animal Intelligence gallery Step into the gallery after storytime to take a selfie with Sammy the Camel! 1-1:30 pm | Wolves 101 Talk Arnold Board Room Learn all about wolves from a globally recognized wolf sanctuary, Wolf Haven International, at this engaging talk for kids! 3-4 pm | Wolves in the Pacific Northwest Talk Arnold Board Room Learn all about wolves of the Pacific Northwest and what it takes to restore near-extinct populations from Wolf Haven International. Sun Apr 12-Sun Apr 19 Can't make the kick-off event on April 11? Visit any open day during spring break week to write to an animal, build a beast and go on a scavenger hunt. Plus, curator Pam McClusky will be leading a few short tours of the exhibition \(3 pm on Apr 15, 16 and 17\) and all public tours that week will stop in A Room for Animal Intelligence. These activities are free to members and 40% off admission for your guests! Simply reserve museum tickets for April 11. Or visit between the 12 and 19 to celebrate A Room for Animal Intelligence with us.

Event details

item.title: Family Saturday | Get Ready for Baby Rave
item.description:
Saturday, April 11, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Olympic Sculpture Park, 2901 Western Ave, Seattle, WA

Gear up for Baby Rave by transforming everyday items into vibrant wearable works of art with guidance from teaching artist, Claudia Villalobos! Upcycle materials including cardboard, fabric scraps, and repurposed embellishments to design wings, crowns, skirts and other wearable pieces that sparkle, reflect light, and show off your family's creativity. Ideal for kids and grown-ups to create together. Then spend your afternoon with your family exploring art and the natural environment. This program takes place outside at the Olympic Sculpture Park on the covered north terrace of the PACCAR Pavilion. Walk-ins welcome on a first-come, first-served basis.

Event details

item.title: Eric-Paul Riege: ojo|-|ólǫ́
item.description:
Saturday, April 11, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

ojo|-|ólǫ́ is an exhibition of recent and newly commissioned work by Diné artist Eric-Paul Riege \(b. 1994, Na’nízhoozhí \[Gallup, New Mexico\]\) that includes sculpture, textile, collage, and video, activated by moments of performance. Across this work, Riege combines customary Diné practices of weaving, silversmithing, and beading with contemporary cultural forms, exploring Diné cosmology, the history of Euro-American trading posts in and adjacent to the Navajo Nation, and the notion of “authenticity” as a value marker of Indigenous art and craft. Developed in partnership between the Henry and The Bell Gallery at Brown University, ojo|-|ólǫ́ emerged from Riege’s material research with Navajo collections housed at Brown’s Haffenreffer Museum of Anthropology and the University of Washington’s Burke Museum of Natural History and Culture. The resulting body of work celebrates the ancestral knowledge and labor contained within Indigenous-made objects, while investigating the role of museums and other institutions in the dissemination and dispossession of knowledge about Indigenous cultures. For his tactile and modular soft sculptures, Riege employs exaggerated scale and combines synthetic and natural materials to playfully question ideas of authenticity and Indigenous artistic production. Processes of making and remaking embody a living practice of exchange that draws together webs of connection across time and space. The exhibition also includes objects from the Burke collection displayed alongside assorted ephemera from Riege’s own home and studio. The resulting non-hierarchal display blurs lines between past and present, private and public, and real and fake, animating the dynamism and dimensionality of Indigenous cultures and identities. ojo|-|ólǫ́ invites collective reflection on the practices of institutions that have accumulated Indigenous art, while advancing a call for Indigenous cultural resurgence in the present and toward our shared future.

Event details

item.title: The 37th Annual Best of the Northwest Spring Show
item.description:
Saturday, April 11, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Magnuson Park Hangar 30, 6310 NE 74th St., Seattle, WA

The 37th Annual Best of the Northwest Spring Show returns to Hangar 30 at Magnuson Park on April 11–12, 2026, featuring over 100 Pacific Northwest artists selling one-of-a-kind paintings, sculpture, ceramics, jewelry, glass, fiber, and more. Enjoy live artist demonstrations, a special exhibit and youth soapstone carving sessions with the Northwest Stone Sculptors Association, local food vendors, and a community art project — all under one roof. Advance tickets are just $5 \($8 at the door\), kids 12 & under are free, and entry covers both days. Free parking and ADA accessible.

Event details

item.title: Youth Art Class: Introduction to Embroidery
item.description:
Saturday, April 11, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: National Nordic Museum, 2655 NW Market Street, Seattle, WA

Learn the basics of hand embroidery in this beginner-friendly youth workshop inspired by classic Nordic floral motifs. Students will practice simple stitches while creating designs based on leaves, flowers, and natural patterns found in Nordic landscapes. No experience necessary; all materials are provided. This hands-on program offers a calm, creative space to slow down, try something new, and leave with a finished piece and skills to continue stitching at home.

Event details

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Saturday, April 11, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

Event details

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Saturday, April 11, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

Event details

item.title: Dragon's Landing Celebration and Treasure Hunt!
item.description:
Sunday, April 12, 2026 at 10 AM

Get ready to celebrate Renton’s rooftop dragon at the 7th Annual Dragon’s Landing Street Fair and Dragon Egg Treasure Hunt! 1️⃣ Dragon Egg Scavenger Hunt Starting April 10, follow the clues, find hidden dragon eggs throughout the city, and log your discoveries for a chance to win amazing dragon treasure! 2️⃣ Student Poster Contest K–12 students are invited to design the official poster for the 7th Annual Dragon’s Landing Event. Winners will have their artwork featured at the event, in the student art show, and online! Submissions due March 6 3️⃣ Window Decorating Contest for Businesses Local businesses are invited to decorate their windows in dragon-inspired creativity! Show off your storefront, engage the community, and add to the festive magic of downtown Renton. ✨ Event Day – Sunday, April 12, 2026 Enjoy captivating performances, creative crafts, and fun for all ages as we come together to celebrate our rooftop dragon! Sign up for treasure hunt updates here: https://forms.gle/Tt9g8KUmmBPthmgV6 More information available at Rentonwa.gov/dragonslanding \(check back for updates!\) Step into a world of enchantment and let your imagination soar!

item.title: Samantha Yun Wall: What We Leave Behind
item.description:
Sunday, April 12, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Samantha Yun Wall’s new paintings use overlapping silhouettes of female figures as portals to unknown spaces and different temporal realms. Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

Event details

item.title: NW Vila Fest 20206
item.description:
Sunday, April 12, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Shoreline Community College Theater, 16101 Greenwood Ave N, Shoreline, WA

Seattle's 3-Day Bellydance Festival is back! Get ready for an unforgettable weekend filled with dynamic workshops, a vibrant vending marketplace, free daytime haflas, delicious food trucks, evening showcases, and most importantly, empowering community connection!   Vending, daytime haflas \(dance performances\), food trucks open to the public.  No entry fees.  See website for prices for evening shows and workshops.

Event details

item.title: Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light
item.description:
Sunday, April 12, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light invites you to become part of the art. Agha, a Pakistani American artist, animates spaces with her large-scale sculptural installations. Suspended from the ceiling, Agha’s steel cubes are laser-cut with intricate designs that project geometric shadows onto the visitor. She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. An in-depth exploration of Islamic architecture, art, and identity, this is the first solo exhibition of a Pakistani American artist in SAM’s 90-year history. Agha is carving a name out for herself in the art world—visit the Seattle Asian Art Museum for an immersive and illuminating experience.

Event details

item.title: Youth Soapstone Carving Workshops At Best of the Northwest Art Show
item.description:
Sunday, April 12, 2026 at 10:30 AM

Location: Magnuson Park Hangar 30, 6310 NE 74th St., Seattle, WA

The Northwest Stone Sculptors Association \(NWSSA\) is excited to offer Youth Soapstone Carving Workshops during the Northwest Art Alliance Best of the Northwest Spring Show on April 11th & 12th at Magnuson Park Hangar 30. Youth can participate an up-close, hands-on experience with creativity by learning to carve their own soapstone animal figurine. Artist instructors from the Northwest Stone Sculptors Association will guide youth participants Ages 7 – 17 through the process of shaping this beautiful, natural material into a figurine to take home. These workshops are designed to promote fine motor skills, 3D spatial reasoning, and artistic confidence. The soapstone and all necessary kid-safe tools and water to minimize dust will be provided. The youth workshops are Free with admission to the Best of the Northwest Show.   Two 90 minute sessions are available, on Saturday April 11th and Sunday, April 12th beginning at 10:30 am and 1:30 pm.  Spots are limited and registration is encouraged.

Event details

Sunday worship Services for the Olympic View Church All are welcome to attend. 17321 44th Avenue West, Lynnwood, WA 98036

Event details

item.title: You've Had It In You All Along
item.description:
Sunday, April 12, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Avery Barnes Gallery, 120 First Ave. S., Seattle, WA

Artists: Barbara Earl Thomas | Tariqa Waters | Cristina Martinez | Robert Wade | Maikoiyo Alley-Barnes | Nahom Ghirmay “ You’ve Had It In You All Along” is an affirmation of our capability to weather any storm. It serves as a vital reminder that the tools required to reach your destination are already etched within your spirit. We already carry the brilliance and the beauty of what it takes—the confidence, the strength, the security, the poise, and the knowledge to achieve our goals. The grit, the rigor, the radical ideas, and the voice required to transcend the world around us are not external gifts to be acquired; they are inherent powers waiting for the moment of their activation.  In the process of overcoming—when the pathway forward is unclear and the effort feels endless—it is easy to lose sight of our own internal compass. When we stand face-to-face with rejection, heartbreak, or the heavy weight of difficult seasons, this exhibition serves as a sanctuary to remind us that we have always been more than enough, exactly as we are. Breakthroughs happen in the space where we are gentle with ourselves, allowing for the activation of an inner wisdom and intuition that has been present since our first breath.

Event details

item.title: Boren Banner Series: Camille Trautman
item.description:
Sunday, April 12, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Frye Art Museum, 704 Terry Ave, Seattle, WA

Every photograph is a reminder that the act of framing is never neutral. In Camille Trautman’s first solo museum exhibition in their hometown, the Seattle-born Duwamish artist uses photography and video to challenge colonial narratives and counter Indigenous erasure. The exhibition presents selections from their ongoing series The North American LCD—spectral self-portraits staged in varied natural landscapes. Rooted in Trautman’s process of coming out as transgender, the images show the artist’s body partially obscured by large LCD screens. The series examines how both landscape photography and digital media can shape or distort cultural identity, often by promoting or denying visibility.

Event details

item.title: EMBODIED ALTARS DANCE CLASS
item.description:
Monday, April 13, 2026 at 6:30 PM

Location: Daybreak Star Indian Cultural Center, 5011 Bernie Whitebear Way, Seattle, WA

EMBODIED ALTARS is an all-levels dance class hosted by Daybreak Star Radio. Designed by Indigenous Chicana artist and El Sueño director, Alicia Mullikin. Dancers will utilize Indigenous principles within movement, meditation, and song as a way to practice gratitude, honor the land, and recognize their ancestors. The class is for those who are interested in reflection, intention setting, and a discovery of their own stories. No dance experience necessary. Class Time: Mondays, 6:30-8PM No class 9/15/25 No class 10/13/25 Venue: Daybreak Star Indian Cultural Center 5011 Bernie Whitebear Way, Seattle, WA 98199 Sliding Scale: $10-$20\* per class. Sign Up Now!   This venue is ADA accessible w/ ADA bathrooms and a wheelchair lift to the second floor. Class will be held in the ballroom space, this area has 5 ADA parking spaces. There are an additional 25 parking spots in our lot, there is ample parking space along the road leading up to Daybreak star as well as the Discovery Park North Parking lot. What to bring: water, comfortable clothes to dance in, journal, your personal identity, a desire to explore new pathways and an open mind/heart. Hosted at Daybreak Star Indian Cultural Center Produced by United Indians of All Tribes Foundation In Partnership with El Sueno Dance & Daybreak Star Radio “Indigenize the Airwaves” “Art. Culture. Reclamation.” \*Talk to us about BIPOC and Queer/LGBTQIA2S\+ Scholorships\* Please contact @el.sueno.dance on IG for private conversations about this topic.

Event details

item.title: Man of La Mancha
item.description:
Monday, April 13, 2026 at 7:30 PM

Location: Theatre Off Jackson, 409 7th Ave S, Seattle, WA

Reboot Theatre Company, in association with Theatre Off Jackson, present an all women/TGNC production of MAN OF LA MANCHA. Directed by Jeannette LeGault, music direction by Aimee Hong , and intimacy and fight choreography by Lee Ann Hittenberger.  Performances run April 3-18, 2026, at Theatre Off Jackson \(409 7th Ave. S. Seattle 98104\) Told through a feminist lens, and performed by an all women/TGNC ensemble, our production of this classic musical explores the ongoing fight for women’s rights, including the pivotal battles of the 21st century. Both urgent and hopeful, this powerful reimagining asks, “What does it mean to dream the impossible dream when your voice, your rights, and your choices are at risk?” Written by Dale Wasserman; Music by Mitch Leigh; Lyrics by Joe Darion; Original production directed by Albert Marre. Man of La Mancha is presented by arrangement with Concord Theatricals on behalf of Tams-Witmark LLC. www.concordtheatricals.com

Event details

item.title: Planet Money Live
item.description:
Monday, April 13, 2026 at 7:30 PM

Location: Town Hall, 1119 Eighth Avenue (at Seneca Street), Seattle, WA

Join the team behind the popular podcast Planet Money and celebrate the publication of their new book, Planet Money: A Guide to the Economic Forces That Shape Your Life. Book author and longtime contributor Alex Mayyasi, Planet Money host Kenny Malone, and visual host Jack Corbett will bring Planet Money’s signature brand of storytelling to the stage and discuss some highlights from the new book, including unlikely origin stories, revealing truths about human behavior, and insightful suggestions for navigating chaotic times drawn from the latest ideas in economics. Daryl Fairweather, Chief Economist at Redfin, will be the special guest this evening. Come prepared to engage with the silly and the sublime and ask some questions of your favorite Planet Money hosts and reporters. Each book purchased with a ticket comes with an exclusive Planet Money tote bag!

Event details

item.title: Gabor Maté
item.description:
Monday, April 13, 2026 at 7:30 PM

Location: McCaw Hall, 321 Mercer Street, Seattle, WA

Embark on a profound journey into the intricate landscapes of the human mind and soul with renowned physician, speaker, and bestselling author, Dr. Gabor Maté. With compassion and unwavering authenticity, Dr. Maté explores the interconnectedness of mind, body, and spirit, shedding light on the underlying causes of human suffering and resilience. Drawing from his groundbreaking research on addiction, trauma, and the impact of early childhood experiences, he offers a transformative perspective on healing and personal growth. Through compelling anecdotes, scientific research, and practical wisdom, Dr. Maté invites audiences to reconsider their understanding of mental health, addiction, and the nature of human connection. Whether you're a healthcare professional, a mental health advocate, or simply a curious seeker of truth, this event promises to inspire, educate, and ignite a deeper understanding of the human condition.

Event details

item.title: Twatties Open Mic of the Absurd!
item.description:
Monday, April 13, 2026 at 8 PM

Location: Saint John's Bar and Eatery, 719 E Pike St, Seattle, WA

Welcome to Twatties! Seattle’s home to the zany and insane in Capitol Hill. We are an absurdist open mic variety show bringing old-Seattle anti-normative joy. We offer performers four minutes to try anything in front of a live and engaging audience.  All performance art such as standup, music, poetry, drag, and puppets is welcomed. We showcase local art installations, a cozy atmosphere, and great food and drink specials to make Twatties the perfect weirdo third place. Interested in performing? Showcasing your work? Or just looking for a great place to see art and connect with a community? Stage time sign-ups fill fast, so arrive at 7:00 P.M. when the list goes live for the best chance to perform. Audience members wishing to witness the cozy chaos must note the show begins at 8:00 P.M. and has no intermission.” Saint John's Bar and Eatery 719 E Pike St, Seattle, WA 98122 Mondays, starting April 6th. Sign-ups 7:00pm Show 8:00pm EVENT IS FREE 21\+ Must show valid ID

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Tuesday, April 14, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

item.title: Meet the Artists of Flight Patterns
item.description:
Tuesday, April 14, 2026 at 6 PM

Location: Lynnwood Event Center, 3711 196th Street SW, Lynnwood, WA

The Lynnwood Event Center at The District warmly invites you to our spring “Meet the Artists” Reception, an evening showcasing our latest art exhibit, “Flight Patterns: The Art & Motion of Winged Life.” Taking place on Tuesday, April 14th from 6-8pm at the Lynnwood Event Center, this is a free and fun community event not to be missed! Guests can meet and mingle with our talented artists and fellow art enthusiasts while enjoying live music, complimentary small bites, and refreshments from the bar. A highlight of the evening will be a live, participatory installation by Alexandra Nason, who will invite guests to decorate individual aluminum butterflies that come together as an evolving work of art. Some artists will also speak and answer questions throughout the evening about their work and creative process. Flight Patterns: The Art & Motion of Winged Life brings together a dynamic collection of works inspired by birds, flight, and the natural rhythms of movement and migration. Through a wide range of mediums and perspectives, the exhibit explores the beauty, symbolism, and energy of winged life. We hope you’ll join us for an evening of art, conversation, and discovery! The event is free with RSVP.

item.title: Type Design Basics
item.description:
Tuesday, April 14, 2026 at 6 PM

Location: Seattle Central College Continuing Education, 1701 Broadway, Seattle, WA

Class Overview So you want to design a typeface? Learn how to hand-draw letters and digitize them to create your own unique functional font! In this practical workshop, students will create an original text typeface based on traditional European calligraphy. Whether you want to develop your typography skills, improve your lettering practice, or become a type designer, learning the fundamentals of type design can bring your skills to the next level. Explore both analog media \(pen on paper\) and digital media \(computer\). Learn how to use a calligraphy pen to develop an understanding of the underlying structure of the Latin alphabet. Then you can work on improving your drawings digitally, and create a typeface using Glyphs \(or the font editor of your choice\).   Class time is dedicated to working on your project with individualized feedback, supplemented by lectures, technical demonstrations, and group critique. By the end of the class, you will have a working text typeface of your own creation, as well as a sketch-to-screen workflow that you can use for future font creation. Students will be encouraged to spend up to 10 hours on homework outside of class time. Designing a typeface is a laborious process, but time flies when experimenting, drawing, and editing! This class is offered in partnership with the amazing Seattle Creative Academy \(but this class is not part of their program and is not for credit/degree\)! See also: Experimental Lettering and other Graphic Design or art classes on our website. Learning Objectives Learn the basics of calligraphy Understand the structure and patterning of the Latin alphabet Create your own original letterforms Digitize and refine your original design Learn how to optimize a typeface for text Become familiar with a sketch-to-screen digital type design workflow Who Might Enjoy This Class? Anyone who wants to develop their own custom font Graphic designers, illustrators, lettering artists, type designers -- anyone with related experience who would like to hone their type design skills Architects, engineers, artists, crafters, writers, book lovers -- anyone interested in the form of letters who would enjoy creating and using their own handmade font No prerequisites, but basic graphic design/typography knowledge is recommended

item.title: Write a Feature Film
item.description:
Tuesday, April 14, 2026 at 6:30 PM

Location: Seattle Central College Continuing Education, 1701 Broadway, Seattle, WA

Class Overview Do you want to write a screenplay for a feature film, but don’t know where to start? Perhaps you’re a beginner curious about the mechanics of screenplays, or a longtime writer feeling a bit stuck, or a director who would enjoy a new perspective. In this class, gain the skills to turn your ideas into a solid screenplay for a feature film. Gain the tools and building blocks to help guide each part of the writing process for a feature-length screenplay. Learn how to be successful with each step, from idea generation, to writing and revising, and especially outlining and structuring your film. Each 90-minute class will include one or more short in-class exercises, from brainstorming to writing and revising scenes. In the sixth and final class, you will have an opportunity to read an excerpt of your script or present a detailed synopsis to classmates. You’ll be encouraged to work between classes. We’ll look at ways to organize your ideas into an outline that can advance progressively closer to a feature film, discussing diverse and alternative approaches in your structure that might help your script connect with audiences without using clichés. Explore how three-dimensional characters drive plot and how setting, staging, and dialogue can be used to tell your story in an energetic, immersive way. We’ll discuss the differences between writing a screenplay you plan to direct yourself versus one you’re writing for someone else.  There will be handouts and/or reading suggestions offered in class. We’ll look at excerpts from books such as Aristotle’s Poetics and Susan Sontag’s Against Interpretation, as well as film clips and interviews with a diverse array of famous filmmakers across time and space. Get support and guidance on how to find inspiration for your own personal artistic expression and the drive to finish your project. Explore the many joys that can be found in this unique, creative, and multifaceted art form. Learning Objectives Brainstorm and organize ideas for a screenplay Approach structure in a flexible way that allows for different types of films Create three-dimensional characters that form the basis of a story How form can be progressive and new, just like content Find your voice and learn to write comfortably and confidently as yourself Who Might Enjoy This Class? Writers interested in exploring how they might approach a screenplay Anyone who wants to develop their voice as a screenwriter and filmmaker People who have begun a feature-length screenplay and would like the support to continue Artists drawn to other parts of the filmmaking process who would like to explore the writing side Anyone interested in how films are structured and what screenplays are made of Filmmakers who want to create an unconventional piece without sacrificing character and structure No experience is needed; everyone is welcome!

JOURNEY is one of the most popular American rock bands of all time, creating some of the best-known songs in modern music. They have continued to tour and record into the 21st century, firing on all cylinders and making sure their fans will never stop believin'. Since the group's formation in 1973, the band has earned 19 Top 40 singles and 25 Gold and Platinum albums. The Greatest Hits album is certified 15 times-Platinum, bringing Journey into the elite club of Diamond-certified holders.

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Wednesday, April 15, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

Wednesday, April 15, 2026 at 10 AM

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. Event details

item.title: Samantha Yun Wall: What We Leave Behind
item.description:
Wednesday, April 15, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Samantha Yun Wall’s new paintings use overlapping silhouettes of female figures as portals to unknown spaces and different temporal realms. Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

Event details

item.title: Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light
item.description:
Wednesday, April 15, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light invites you to become part of the art. Agha, a Pakistani American artist, animates spaces with her large-scale sculptural installations. Suspended from the ceiling, Agha’s steel cubes are laser-cut with intricate designs that project geometric shadows onto the visitor. She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. An in-depth exploration of Islamic architecture, art, and identity, this is the first solo exhibition of a Pakistani American artist in SAM’s 90-year history. Agha is carving a name out for herself in the art world—visit the Seattle Asian Art Museum for an immersive and illuminating experience.

Event details

item.title: You've Had It In You All Along
item.description:
Wednesday, April 15, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Avery Barnes Gallery, 120 First Ave. S., Seattle, WA

Artists: Barbara Earl Thomas | Tariqa Waters | Cristina Martinez | Robert Wade | Maikoiyo Alley-Barnes | Nahom Ghirmay “ You’ve Had It In You All Along” is an affirmation of our capability to weather any storm. It serves as a vital reminder that the tools required to reach your destination are already etched within your spirit. We already carry the brilliance and the beauty of what it takes—the confidence, the strength, the security, the poise, and the knowledge to achieve our goals. The grit, the rigor, the radical ideas, and the voice required to transcend the world around us are not external gifts to be acquired; they are inherent powers waiting for the moment of their activation.  In the process of overcoming—when the pathway forward is unclear and the effort feels endless—it is easy to lose sight of our own internal compass. When we stand face-to-face with rejection, heartbreak, or the heavy weight of difficult seasons, this exhibition serves as a sanctuary to remind us that we have always been more than enough, exactly as we are. Breakthroughs happen in the space where we are gentle with ourselves, allowing for the activation of an inner wisdom and intuition that has been present since our first breath.

item.title: SP 101: Intro to Vera's Screen Print Studio
item.description:
Wednesday, April 15, 2026 at 6 PM

Location: Book-It Repertory Theatre, 305 Harrison Street, Seattle, WA

This 2-hour, comprehensive hands-on class teaches you all the basics of screen printing at VERA’s screen printing studio: From hand-drawing a stencil on transparency, coating and burning a screen, to pulling your very first print! Get comfortable with Vera's shop layout, equipment, and culture. Learn techniques for creating screens, terminology, and more to create 1-color projects in the print shop. Students will walk away with the basic skills for screen printing 1-color paper prints, and their own print of a special Vera design, specific to the 101 class. This class is a prerequisite for 200-level classes at VERA, as well as access to Open Sessions to work on your own projects in VERA’s screen printing studio.

item.title: Eric-Paul Riege: ojo|-|ólǫ́
item.description:
Thursday, April 16, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

ojo|-|ólǫ́ is an exhibition of recent and newly commissioned work by Diné artist Eric-Paul Riege \(b. 1994, Na’nízhoozhí \[Gallup, New Mexico\]\) that includes sculpture, textile, collage, and video, activated by moments of performance. Across this work, Riege combines customary Diné practices of weaving, silversmithing, and beading with contemporary cultural forms, exploring Diné cosmology, the history of Euro-American trading posts in and adjacent to the Navajo Nation, and the notion of “authenticity” as a value marker of Indigenous art and craft. Developed in partnership between the Henry and The Bell Gallery at Brown University, ojo|-|ólǫ́ emerged from Riege’s material research with Navajo collections housed at Brown’s Haffenreffer Museum of Anthropology and the University of Washington’s Burke Museum of Natural History and Culture. The resulting body of work celebrates the ancestral knowledge and labor contained within Indigenous-made objects, while investigating the role of museums and other institutions in the dissemination and dispossession of knowledge about Indigenous cultures. For his tactile and modular soft sculptures, Riege employs exaggerated scale and combines synthetic and natural materials to playfully question ideas of authenticity and Indigenous artistic production. Processes of making and remaking embody a living practice of exchange that draws together webs of connection across time and space. The exhibition also includes objects from the Burke collection displayed alongside assorted ephemera from Riege’s own home and studio. The resulting non-hierarchal display blurs lines between past and present, private and public, and real and fake, animating the dynamism and dimensionality of Indigenous cultures and identities. ojo|-|ólǫ́ invites collective reflection on the practices of institutions that have accumulated Indigenous art, while advancing a call for Indigenous cultural resurgence in the present and toward our shared future.

Event details

item.title: Earth Month Event | Nature Journaling
item.description:
Thursday, April 16, 2026 at 10 AM

Nature Journaling in Pioneer Park It's Earth Month! And to celebrate we're hosting the first of our seasonal Nature Journaling educa tional events. We will be exploring Pioneer Park with notebooks and pens m aking observations about what we see. Participants will get instructions o n how to begin nature journaling as well as a series of prompts to help gu ide your observations. What is nature journaling? It is a practice in which you observe nature and spend some time crafting notes whether that' s words, drawings, painting, quantitative data, etc. Click here to learn more: https://johnmuirlaws.com/nature-journaling-starting-growing / All ages welcome, no experience necessary. When: Thursday, April 16th, 10am-12pm Where: Pioneer Park NW, Meet at the corner of Island Crest Way and SE 68th St. Follow VOLUNTEER EVENT Signs What to bring: Dr ess for the weather in comfortable clothes. Please bring a notebook, and pens/pencils/water colors/crayons/etc Registration: https://volunteersig nup.org/J7KYK

She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. An in-depth exploration of Islamic architecture, art, and identity, this is the first solo exhibition of a Pakistani American artist in SAM’s 90-year history. Agha is carving a name out for herself in the art world—visit the Seattle Asian Art Museum for an immersive and illuminating experience.

Event details

item.title: You've Had It In You All Along
item.description:
Friday, April 17, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Avery Barnes Gallery, 120 First Ave. S., Seattle, WA

Artists: Barbara Earl Thomas | Tariqa Waters | Cristina Martinez | Robert Wade | Maikoiyo Alley-Barnes | Nahom Ghirmay “ You’ve Had It In You All Along” is an affirmation of our capability to weather any storm. It serves as a vital reminder that the tools required to reach your destination are already etched within your spirit. We already carry the brilliance and the beauty of what it takes—the confidence, the strength, the security, the poise, and the knowledge to achieve our goals. The grit, the rigor, the radical ideas, and the voice required to transcend the world around us are not external gifts to be acquired; they are inherent powers waiting for the moment of their activation.  In the process of overcoming—when the pathway forward is unclear and the effort feels endless—it is easy to lose sight of our own internal compass. When we stand face-to-face with rejection, heartbreak, or the heavy weight of difficult seasons, this exhibition serves as a sanctuary to remind us that we have always been more than enough, exactly as we are. Breakthroughs happen in the space where we are gentle with ourselves, allowing for the activation of an inner wisdom and intuition that has been present since our first breath.

Event details

item.title: Decay
item.description:
Friday, April 17, 2026 at 12 PM

Location: The Vestibule, 5919 15th Ave. N.W., Seattle, WA

Decay explores how we navigate and engage with the inevitable entropy of our world. This exhibition shows us the joy of degeneration, the beauty of collapse, the peace that comes from crumbling into dust. The work, primarily from Pacific Northwest artists, ranges from sculptural, to wall-mounted 3D, video and installation works. Included in this exhibition will be works created using natural and reclaimed materials, as well as pieces designed to create spaces for quiet reflection on the theme. Decay appears as part of the natural world and in our bodies in many forms, and so over the duration of the show, some works may change over time, exemplifying the theme. Many of the pieces prompt reflection on mortality, the fragility of the physical body and the sacred.

Event details

item.title: Scott Gibson - Visual Symphony II
item.description:
Friday, April 17, 2026 at 12 PM

Location: Gray Sky Gallery, 320 First Ave. S., Seattle, WA

In March, Visual Symphony II Scott Gibson invites viewers into a world where music and art collide. Each work is an exploration of the vivid, sensory experience of sound translating into vibrant, emotional visuals. As an artist with synesthesia each work expresses Scott’s personal perception of what music looks like. Scott uses resin techniques that he has pioneered and pushes the boundaries of this medium.

Event details

item.title: Eric-Paul Riege: ojo|-|ólǫ́
item.description:
Saturday, April 18, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

ojo|-|ólǫ́ is an exhibition of recent and newly commissioned work by Diné artist Eric-Paul Riege \(b. 1994, Na’nízhoozhí \[Gallup, New Mexico\]\) that includes sculpture, textile, collage, and video, activated by moments of performance. Across this work, Riege combines customary Diné practices of weaving, silversmithing, and beading with contemporary cultural forms, exploring Diné cosmology, the history of Euro-American trading posts in and adjacent to the Navajo Nation, and the notion of “authenticity” as a value marker of Indigenous art and craft. Developed in partnership between the Henry and The Bell Gallery at Brown University, ojo|-|ólǫ́ emerged from Riege’s material research with Navajo collections housed at Brown’s Haffenreffer Museum of Anthropology and the University of Washington’s Burke Museum of Natural History and Culture. The resulting body of work celebrates the ancestral knowledge and labor contained within Indigenous-made objects, while investigating the role of museums and other institutions in the dissemination and dispossession of knowledge about Indigenous cultures. For his tactile and modular soft sculptures, Riege employs exaggerated scale and combines synthetic and natural materials to playfully question ideas of authenticity and Indigenous artistic production. Processes of making and remaking embody a living practice of exchange that draws together webs of connection across time and space. The exhibition also includes objects from the Burke collection displayed alongside assorted ephemera from Riege’s own home and studio. The resulting non-hierarchal display blurs lines between past and present, private and public, and real and fake, animating the dynamism and dimensionality of Indigenous cultures and identities. ojo|-|ólǫ́ invites collective reflection on the practices of institutions that have accumulated Indigenous art, while advancing a call for Indigenous cultural resurgence in the present and toward our shared future.

Event details

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Saturday, April 18, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

Event details

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Sunday, April 19, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

Event details

item.title: Samantha Yun Wall: What We Leave Behind
item.description:
Sunday, April 19, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Samantha Yun Wall’s new paintings use overlapping silhouettes of female figures as portals to unknown spaces and different temporal realms. Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

item.title: Ragamala Indian Classical Music and Arts Celebration
item.description:
Sunday, April 19, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Olympic Sculpture Park, 2901 Western Ave, Seattle, WA

Join Ragamala Seattle for a family-friendly day of Indian music and art activities at the Seattle Asian Art Museum. 11 am-noon Enjoy the beats and rhythms of Indian classical music by participating in a drum circle led by Ravi Albright of the Seattle Tabla Institute. noon-1 pm Learn to sing Hindustani vocal music in this beginner-friendly workshop led by teaching artists Suchitra Iyer and Anindita Das 2-4 pm Enjoy a concert and performance from celebrated Sitar player Pt. Kushal Das accompanied by Shri. Sandip Ghosh on Tabla. Pt. Kushal Das, renowned Sitar and Surbahar artist, was born in a musical family in Kolkata. His father Sailen Das was a disciple of Lakshman Bhattacharya and Pt. Ravi Shankar. His uncle Santanu Das was a disciple of Pt. Ali Akbar Khan. He started his training under the guidance of his father and uncle. Later he received training from Sanjoy Bandopadhyay and from Ajoy Sinha Roy \(a disciple of Pt. Allauddin Khan\) and took vocal lessons from Pt. Manas Chakraborty and Pt. Ramkrishna Basu. Pt. Das will be accompanied on the tabla by Sandip Ghosh, who has been a disciple of Pt. Anindo Chatterjee for the last 18 years. Sandip has accompanied distinguished artists like Pt. Ajoy Chakrabarty, Ud. Shahid Parvez, Ud. Shujaat Khan, Pt. Hari Prasad Chaurasia and Vid. Kaushiki Chakrabarty. He is also a solo artist, collaborating with bands worldwide.

item.title: Paella Pop-up at a/stir
item.description:
Monday, April 20, 2026 at 6 PM

Location: a/stir, 818 E Pike St, Seattle, WA

You know us \(or maybe you don't\). We are obssessed with Northern Mediterranean cuisine. What is more quintessential than Paella when it comes to Northern Mediterranean? \(Well, a few other dishes but paella is certainly one of them\). So, join us for a very limited pop-up for a seafood paella pop-up at a/stir. The ONLY option for dinner is, you guessed it, paella. What is in it? This one will feature smoked chicken thighs, mussels, shrimp and chorizo \(no, we won't forget the saffron, onion and the bell peppers\). No vegan or vegetarian options this time around. What else comes with it? A glass of Spanish wine - white or red, your choice. Anything else? Well, if you so choose, you will be able to order a side salad and a basque cheesecake as dessert \(neither are included in price, and both in very limited quantities\). Seatings at 6pm, 7pm or 8pm. Choose carefully. :-\) Price? $50/person, inclusive of taxes and gratuity. Includes a generous portion of paella and a 5 oz glass of delicious Spanish wine. Salad and Basque cheesecake optionally available at the restaurant at time of dinner. FINE PRINT Since this is a pop-up, we cannot make any accomodations and substitutions with food content. There will NOT be any other regular menu items on the day of service - this is a pop-up for PAELLA only. Not a drinker? Don't wanna pay for the wine? Consider joining us at another pop-up event. Since this is such a limited and narrow scope engagement, we are not in a position to accomodate requests.

item.title: Free Throw
item.description:
Monday, April 20, 2026 at 6:30 PM

Location: The Showbox, 1426 1st Ave., Seattle, WA

Since forming in 2012, FREE THROW's story has been simple: keep going. New records, longer tours, bigger rooms - it's always been another step upward for the Nashville-based quintet, whose 2014 debut, Those Days Are Gone, has become an era-defining classic in the emo-punk genre, launching them onto global tours with Hot Mulligan and New Found Glory and slots at Slam Dunk, Riot Fest, and The Fest. Every milestone has pushed them ahead, but on their sixth LP, Moments Before The Wind \(Wax Bodega\), that constant charge gradually slows, suspended mid-step, mid-thought, mid-life. Recorded with longtime producer Brett Romnes on either end of the sold-out Those Days Are Gone 10-year tour, the follow-up to 2023's Lessons That We Swear To Keep chronicles a surrealist descent into liminality: a period of intense personal upheaval colored by loss, renewal, reflection, and, ultimately, life-altering joy through the impending birth of a child. The 11-song set is disarmingly real, colored by sonic spontaneity - a swirl of Midwest emo, sweat-soaked punk fervor, and crashing alternative rock - but also the sort of achingly vulnerable lyricism fans and critics have come to expect from the band. Through it all, Moments finds Free Throw still moving, still growing, but now, perhaps more than ever, acutely aware that the path ahead is neither straight nor obvious. There's a quiet confidence guiding them there into a brand-new era, ready for whatever the wind blows their way.

Event details

item.title: EMBODIED ALTARS DANCE CLASS
item.description:
Monday, April 20, 2026 at 6:30 PM

Location: Daybreak Star Indian Cultural Center, 5011 Bernie Whitebear Way, Seattle, WA

EMBODIED ALTARS is an all-levels dance class hosted by Daybreak Star Radio. Designed by Indigenous Chicana artist and El Sueño director, Alicia Mullikin. Dancers will utilize Indigenous principles within movement, meditation, and song as a way to practice gratitude, honor the land, and recognize their ancestors. The class is for those who are interested in reflection, intention setting, and a discovery of their own stories. No dance experience necessary. Class Time: Mondays, 6:30-8PM No class 9/15/25 No class 10/13/25 Venue: Daybreak Star Indian Cultural Center 5011 Bernie Whitebear Way, Seattle, WA 98199 Sliding Scale: $10-$20\* per class. Sign Up Now!   This venue is ADA accessible w/ ADA bathrooms and a wheelchair lift to the second floor. Class will be held in the ballroom space, this area has 5 ADA parking spaces. There are an additional 25 parking spots in our lot, there is ample parking space along the road leading up to Daybreak star as well as the Discovery Park North Parking lot. What to bring: water, comfortable clothes to dance in, journal, your personal identity, a desire to explore new pathways and an open mind/heart. Hosted at Daybreak Star Indian Cultural Center Produced by United Indians of All Tribes Foundation In Partnership with El Sueno Dance & Daybreak Star Radio “Indigenize the Airwaves” “Art. Culture. Reclamation.” \*Talk to us about BIPOC and Queer/LGBTQIA2S\+ Scholorships\* Please contact @el.sueno.dance on IG for private conversations about this topic.

item.title: Screen Printing Open Session
item.description:
Tuesday, April 21, 2026 at 4 PM

Location: Book-It Repertory Theatre, 305 Harrison Street, Seattle, WA

You MUST take the SP 101: Intro to Vera's Screen Print Studio class before you can sign up for an Open Session\\\*\*\* Vera’s screen print studio offers four-hour Open Sessions where individuals who have completed the introductory class may use our screen print lab to work on their own projects. Our Open Sessions are looked over by a volunteer moderator, who can provide you with advice and guidance for anything taught in our Screen Printing 101 class. We offer three different kinds of tickets at Open Sessions: Vacuum Table for paper and other flat media, T-shirt Press for shirts, tote bags and other fabrics, and Screen prep for just screen preparation. Each ticket is limited to two. If the ticket you are looking for is sold out, please add yourself to the waitlist so you can be notified when a ticket becomes available. For Open Sessions, Vera provides: - Rentable screens - Emulsion and emulsion remover - Squegees - Basic tools like spatulas, rulers, tape, etc. Participants must provide their own: - Substrate \(paper, t-shirts, etc\) - Water based ink Vera \*does not\* allow the use of non-water based inks at our studio, as our studio is not set up to safely process non-water based ink. If you have any questions, please email matt@theveraproject.org. \*\*FAQ\*\* 1. Can Vera print a transparent stencil for me? Yes — You must send us your stencil at least 24 hours before the start of your session. Your file must be a .PDF, no larger than 12" x 18", and ready to print. Vera cannot make any edits to your file. Stencils are $5 each and can be paid via Debit/Credit when you arrive. If you are not interested in getting a stencil printed at Vera, you can print your own on transparency at a FedEx or similar print service location. 2. What if I need direct assistance on my projects? Our moderators are trained to assist with any topics covered in the SP 101 class offered at Vera. While some may be able to give you basic advice on your project, they are not expected to ask as a direct instructor. If you have a project where you would like one-on-one guidance, please reach out to matt@theveraproject.org to request private tutoring. 3. I really want to use your studio during an Open Session, but there are no slots available? Our studio is in high demand, so we apologize if our Open Session is sold out — We have limited space in the studio and several events every day at Vera, so it can be difficult for us to find more time for Open Sessions. If you're interested in booking the studio outside of Open Sessions, please email matt@theveraproject.org to discuss private rental rates and availability.

item.title: Type Design Basics
item.description:
Tuesday, April 21, 2026 at 6 PM

Location: Seattle Central College Continuing Education, 1701 Broadway, Seattle, WA

Class Overview So you want to design a typeface? Learn how to hand-draw letters and digitize them to create your own unique functional font! In this practical workshop, students will create an original text typeface based on traditional European calligraphy. Whether you want to develop your typography skills, improve your lettering practice, or become a type designer, learning the fundamentals of type design can bring your skills to the next level. Explore both analog media \(pen on paper\) and digital media \(computer\). Learn how to use a calligraphy pen to develop an understanding of the underlying structure of the Latin alphabet. Then you can work on improving your drawings digitally, and create a typeface using Glyphs \(or the font editor of your choice\).   Class time is dedicated to working on your project with individualized feedback, supplemented by lectures, technical demonstrations, and group critique. By the end of the class, you will have a working text typeface of your own creation, as well as a sketch-to-screen workflow that you can use for future font creation. Students will be encouraged to spend up to 10 hours on homework outside of class time. Designing a typeface is a laborious process, but time flies when experimenting, drawing, and editing! This class is offered in partnership with the amazing Seattle Creative Academy \(but this class is not part of their program and is not for credit/degree\)! See also: Experimental Lettering and other Graphic Design or art classes on our website. Learning Objectives Learn the basics of calligraphy Understand the structure and patterning of the Latin alphabet Create your own original letterforms Digitize and refine your original design Learn how to optimize a typeface for text Become familiar with a sketch-to-screen digital type design workflow Who Might Enjoy This Class? Anyone who wants to develop their own custom font Graphic designers, illustrators, lettering artists, type designers -- anyone with related experience who would like to hone their type design skills Architects, engineers, artists, crafters, writers, book lovers -- anyone interested in the form of letters who would enjoy creating and using their own handmade font No prerequisites, but basic graphic design/typography knowledge is recommended

Event details

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Wednesday, April 22, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

Event details

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Wednesday, April 22, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

Event details

item.title: Samantha Yun Wall: What We Leave Behind
item.description:
Wednesday, April 22, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Samantha Yun Wall’s new paintings use overlapping silhouettes of female figures as portals to unknown spaces and different temporal realms. Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

Event details

item.title: You've Had It In You All Along
item.description:
Wednesday, April 22, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Avery Barnes Gallery, 120 First Ave. S., Seattle, WA

Artists: Barbara Earl Thomas | Tariqa Waters | Cristina Martinez | Robert Wade | Maikoiyo Alley-Barnes | Nahom Ghirmay “ You’ve Had It In You All Along” is an affirmation of our capability to weather any storm. It serves as a vital reminder that the tools required to reach your destination are already etched within your spirit. We already carry the brilliance and the beauty of what it takes—the confidence, the strength, the security, the poise, and the knowledge to achieve our goals. The grit, the rigor, the radical ideas, and the voice required to transcend the world around us are not external gifts to be acquired; they are inherent powers waiting for the moment of their activation.  In the process of overcoming—when the pathway forward is unclear and the effort feels endless—it is easy to lose sight of our own internal compass. When we stand face-to-face with rejection, heartbreak, or the heavy weight of difficult seasons, this exhibition serves as a sanctuary to remind us that we have always been more than enough, exactly as we are. Breakthroughs happen in the space where we are gentle with ourselves, allowing for the activation of an inner wisdom and intuition that has been present since our first breath.

Event details

item.title: Earth Month Event | Find the ART in E(ART)H Day!
item.description:
Wednesday, April 22, 2026 at 1 PM

Find the ART in E\(ART\)H Day! To celebrate Earth Day, we're hosting a fun weeding and art making class! Join us while we walk throug h the park teaching participants how to identify and remove common weeds. Once we've strolled through the park, we'll spend the remainder of the event crafting with the weeds we removed. If you're interested in natur e crafting, finding ways to make weeding fun, and being creative in the garden, then this is the event for you! No experience necessary, we will provide all the tools and instructions. When: Wednesday, April 22nd, 1-3pm Where: Clarke Beach Park, meet in the parking lot What to bring: Dress for the weather in clothes you're comfortable walking in, and don't mind getting dirty in. Registration: https://volunteersignup.org/7CA DJ

item.title: 2026 Education & Workforce Summit: King County Career Showcase
item.description:
Thursday, April 23, 2026 at 7:30 AM

Location: ShoWare Center, 625 West James Street, Kent, WA

The 2026 Education & Workforce Summit: King County Career Showcase brings together educators, employers, industry leaders, and community partners for a dynamic, future-focused event centered on building a strong regional talent pipeline. This annual summit highlights emerging workforce trends, innovative education strategies, and the evolving needs of King County's key industries. Participants will have the opportunity to explore interactive exhibits, hear from expert panelists, engage in solution-driven discussions, and connect directly with organizations committed to developing the next generation of talent. From K-12 and higher education to workforce development programs and employers, the Summit creates a space where collaboration drives meaningful impact. Join us as we showcase new career pathways, celebrate partnerships, and work together to equip King County residents with the skills and opportunities needed to thrive in a rapidly changing workforce landscape.

item.title: Eric-Paul Riege: ojo|-|ólǫ́
item.description:
Thursday, April 23, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

ojo|-|ólǫ́ is an exhibition of recent and newly commissioned work by Diné artist Eric-Paul Riege \(b. 1994, Na’nízhoozhí \[Gallup, New Mexico\]\) that includes sculpture, textile, collage, and video, activated by moments of performance. Across this work, Riege combines customary Diné practices of weaving, silversmithing, and beading with contemporary cultural forms, exploring Diné cosmology, the history of Euro-American trading posts in and adjacent to the Navajo Nation, and the notion of “authenticity” as a value marker of Indigenous art and craft. Developed in partnership between the Henry and The Bell Gallery at Brown University, ojo|-|ólǫ́ emerged from Riege’s material research with Navajo collections housed at Brown’s Haffenreffer Museum of Anthropology and the University of Washington’s Burke Museum of Natural History and Culture. The resulting body of work celebrates the ancestral knowledge and labor contained within Indigenous-made objects, while investigating the role of museums and other institutions in the dissemination and dispossession of knowledge about Indigenous cultures. For his tactile and modular soft sculptures, Riege employs exaggerated scale and combines synthetic and natural materials to playfully question ideas of authenticity and Indigenous artistic production. Processes of making and remaking embody a living practice of exchange that draws together webs of connection across time and space. The exhibition also includes objects from the Burke collection displayed alongside assorted ephemera from Riege’s own home and studio. The resulting non-hierarchal display blurs lines between past and present, private and public, and real and fake, animating the dynamism and dimensionality of Indigenous cultures and identities. ojo|-|ólǫ́ invites collective reflection on the practices of institutions that have accumulated Indigenous art, while advancing a call for Indigenous cultural resurgence in the present and toward our shared future.

item.title: Scott Gibson - Visual Symphony II
item.description:
Thursday, April 23, 2026 at 12 PM

Location: Gray Sky Gallery, 320 First Ave. S., Seattle, WA

In March, Visual Symphony II Scott Gibson invites viewers into a world where music and art collide. Each work is an exploration of the vivid, sensory experience of sound translating into vibrant, emotional visuals. As an artist with synesthesia each work expresses Scott’s personal perception of what music looks like. Scott uses resin techniques that he has pioneered and pushes the boundaries of this medium.

item.title: Contemporary Dance for Every Body
item.description:
Thursday, April 23, 2026 at 4 PM

Location: Seattle Central College Continuing Education, 1701 Broadway, Seattle, WA

Class Overview Would you like to cultivate body awareness through movement? Explore a grounded and natural approach to moving your body, led by a contemporary dance instructor. Through floorwork, movement sequences, and phrasing, this class cultivates body awareness. Explore efficiency in movement transitions. Develop a deeper understanding of how your body moves naturally in relation to the floor, emphasizing organic flow, continuity, spirality, softness, core engagement, and the intelligent use of gravity. Open your mind and body through guided movement! Explore points of support, releasing tension, and engaging the core to facilitate flow and prevent injury. Movement principles will draw from contemporary dance and somatic practices, encouraging dancers to move with clarity, sensitivity, and presence. This class is for dancers, actors, physical theater artists, yoga/movement practitioners, and anyone interested in exploring movement in their bodies -- no experience necessary. Designed to be inclusive, this class offers both accessibility and challenge to movers at various levels and all backgrounds.  Learning Objectives Understand and embody key principles of movement floorwork, including gravity, spirality, and core initiation. Improve movement continuity and flow between levels \(floor to standing and vice versa\). Explore softness and efficiency through core engagement. Identify and use anatomical points of support for safe and fluid movement. Develop new dance tools Feel more connected with the possibilities of your body in a conscious way to live a more present, healthy, and aware life. Who Might Enjoy This Class? Adult learners of all backgrounds \(18\+\), including dancers, actors, physical theater artists, yoga/movement practitioners Anyone interested in embodied movement practices. Some prior movement or dance experience is helpful but not required. The class will be structured to accommodate varying levels of experience and physical ability.

Event details

item.title: Decay
item.description:
Friday, April 24, 2026 at 12 PM

Location: The Vestibule, 5919 15th Ave. N.W., Seattle, WA

Decay explores how we navigate and engage with the inevitable entropy of our world. This exhibition shows us the joy of degeneration, the beauty of collapse, the peace that comes from crumbling into dust. The work, primarily from Pacific Northwest artists, ranges from sculptural, to wall-mounted 3D, video and installation works. Included in this exhibition will be works created using natural and reclaimed materials, as well as pieces designed to create spaces for quiet reflection on the theme. Decay appears as part of the natural world and in our bodies in many forms, and so over the duration of the show, some works may change over time, exemplifying the theme. Many of the pieces prompt reflection on mortality, the fragility of the physical body and the sacred.

Event details

item.title: Live Music Happy Hour Joe Casalini Trio
item.description:
Friday, April 24, 2026 at 4 PM

Location: The Royal Room, 5000 Rainier Ave South, Seattle, WA

Join us for Live Music Happy Hour with the Joe Casalini Trio. The Casalini Trio has played happy hours at the Royal Room since 2019. They play laid-back, swinging versions of great tunes by Duke Ellington, Billy Strayhorn, Hoagie Carmichael, Phillip Johnston, and other great American songwriters of the last 100 years. The happy hour usually lasts about an hour and a half. Good drinks, good people, good ambience, and there’s never a cover charge, although the band often encourages donations to local food banks and other worthy causes.
